title: Markets Brace for the Triple Threat: CPI, Oracle Earnings, and the SpaceX IPO
subtitle: After Friday's $1.3 trillion wipeout, Wall Street faces a make-or-break week with the May inflation report, Oracle results, and the most hyped IPO in history — all converging in a five-day stretch.
summary: The Nasdaq enters the week nursing a 4.18% bruising from Friday's AI rout. With May CPI due Wednesday, Oracle earnings, and the SpaceX IPO on Friday — this could be the most consequential week of 2026 for tech investors.

image.caption: Wall Street traders prepare for a high-stakes week as CPI data, Oracle earnings, and the SpaceX IPO converge.

bodyHtml:
Friday, June 5, 2026, delivered the kind of session that scars portfolios. The Nasdaq Composite cratered 4.18% to 25,709.43 — its worst single-day plunge since April 2025. The S&P 500 shed 2.64% to 7,383.74, and the Dow surrendered 1.35% to 50,866.78. Across two sessions, $1.3 trillion in market value was erased. This was a liquidation, not a correction.

Two culprits, perfectly timed. Broadcom reported AI guidance that humbled the Street, hemorrhaging roughly $300 billion in market cap and dragging the SOXX semiconductor ETF down 10.4%. Then the May jobs report landed hot: +172,000 payrolls, unemployment at 4.3%, wage growth sticky. Rate-cut hopes collapsed. Futures flipped from pricing cuts to a December hike. Nvidia dropped 6% not because it broke, but because when momentum shatters, even the best names get tossed.

Now it's Monday. The screens are dark. This is a make-or-break week.

The Week's Three Catalysts

Catalyst One: May CPI — Wednesday, June 10. This is the inflation report that could kill whatever remains of the rate-cut narrative. The market now prices a Fed that might hike, not ease. If core CPI prints above 0.3% month-over-month, the bond market punishes equities with a fury that makes Friday look like a warm-up. Consensus expects core CPI at 0.2% month-over-month, year-over-year at 3.3%. Miss that, and everything reprices instantly. A cool print could trigger the kind of violent relief rally that only follows 4%+ wipeouts. Binary event, binary outcome.

Catalyst Two: Oracle Earnings — This Week. Oracle doesn't typically move markets, but this quarter is different. Its cloud infrastructure business has been the dark horse of enterprise AI, quietly signing nine-figure deals with companies training models outside the hyperscaler orbit. Strong cloud revenue and raised AI pipeline guidance would be desperately needed proof that enterprise AI demand is real, not just a chipmaker's fever dream. A whiff, and the "AI capex peak" narrative gains terrifying momentum.

Catalyst Three: The SpaceX IPO — Friday, June 12. The most hyped IPO in history lands Friday. SpaceX priced at $75 billion on the Nasdaq. This is a liquidity event of extraordinary magnitude — institutional investors sell other things to fund a name this big. Portfolio managers trimming Nvidia and Broadcom for SPCX could add selling pressure. But if SPCX opens strong, space and defense plays like Rocket Lab could catch sympathy bids as the narrative shifts from "AI is dead" to "the next frontier is here."

Technical Damage Assessment

The QQQ sliced through its 50-day moving average Friday. Next support: the 100-day near 24,800 — 3.5% lower. Below that, the 200-day around 23,500, a 9% peak-to-trough drawdown. Semiconductors were the epicenter: SOXX -10.4% over two sessions is a move that occurs less than 2% of the time. That's panic.

The semis led the market up for 18 months. If they're now leading it down, the AI trade is being repriced, not just paused.

What held up? Defense. RTX and Lockheed Martin barely flinched. The Global X Defense ETF squeezed out a gain. Small caps showed relative strength too — the Russell 2000 held up markedly better than mega-cap growth. When money rotates from AI momentum into defense value, the regime change is real.

The Bull Case Nobody's Talking About

Markets bounce after 4%+ Nasdaq drops. Since 2010, the index has suffered 15 such days; in 12 cases, it was higher one month later — average forward return +6.2%. Liquidation flushes weak hands, positioning cleans up, and remaining capital has higher conviction.

Friday's selloff was driven disproportionately by systematic strategies — CTAs, vol-targeting funds, risk-parity desks — that sell because volatility spiked, not because the thesis broke. Those forced sellers are mostly done. Sunday night futures were essentially flat (ES near 7,398), suggesting panic has stabilized.

And the SpaceX IPO? A hot debut in a high-imagination sector can reignite animal spirits fast. If SPCX opens above $75 billion Friday, the narrative pivots: "the innovation trade isn't dead, it's finding new names." Rocket Lab and the space/defense complex would be immediate beneficiaries.

The Signal's Take

We're cautious but not panicked. The AI thesis isn't broken — Broadcom's miss is one company's guidance question, not a verdict on the trillion-dollar AI infrastructure buildout still in early innings. Nvidia's Blackwell ramp is accelerating, hyperscaler capex budgets for 2027 are being drafted higher, and enterprise AI adoption is broadening. Those structural tailwinds survive one bad jobs report and one guidance whiff.

But the near-term path is treacherous. Tuesday is critical — if semis stabilize, Friday's panic has exhausted itself and Wednesday's CPI becomes the binary event that defines June. If CPI cooperates, this selloff is a buying opportunity. If it doesn't, the 200-day on QQQ becomes the next chapter.

Watch semiconductors first, bonds second, the SpaceX order book third. The week ahead isn't for the faint of heart — but it's exactly the kind of week where fortunes are made by those who keep their heads.

— The Signal
